original_text: The U.S. dollar strengthens by 10% against major currencies, reducing export competitiveness.
identified_property: magnitude of dollar strengthening and resulting competitiveness loss

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: The U.S. dollar strengthens by 30% against major currencies, devastating export competitiveness and rendering American goods largely uncompetitive in global markets.

variations.label: low
variations.text: The U.S. dollar strengthens by 20% against major currencies, severely undermining export competitiveness and triggering sharp declines in overseas sales.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: The U.S. dollar strengthens by 15% against major currencies, significantly reducing export competitiveness and putting notable pressure on trade-dependent industries.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: The U.S. dollar strengthens by 10% against major currencies, reducing export competitiveness.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: The U.S. dollar strengthens by 7% against major currencies, moderately reducing export competitiveness for some trade-sensitive sectors.

variations.label: high
variations.text: The U.S. dollar strengthens by 4% against major currencies, slightly reducing export competitiveness with limited impact on most exporters.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: The U.S. dollar strengthens by 2% against major currencies, marginally affecting export competitiveness with negligible impact on trade flows.

quality_checks.reasoning: Each tier varies the magnitude of dollar appreciation (30% → 20% → 15% → 10% → 7% → 4% → 2%) with correspondingly scaled language describing the severity of the competitiveness loss (devastating → severely undermining → significantly reducing → reducing → moderately reducing → slightly reducing → marginally affecting). All tiers describe the same core scenario of dollar strengthening against major currencies affecting export competitiveness.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because a mere 2% dollar appreciation with negligible trade impact is the least harmful outcome. A rational agent prefers minimal currency disruption and minimal loss of export competitiveness, making smaller magnitudes of strengthening strictly preferable to larger ones.
